query: most common neonatal fungal infection caused by candida species

retrieved_docs.title: Candidiasis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant women have higher chances of colonizing Candida in the vagina during pregnancy, [12] Vaginal candidiasis is among the common forms of fungal diseases frequently occurring in pregnant women which may lead to systemic infections in neonates, especially with low birth weight (LBW) and prematurity after delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Candidiasis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Neonates affected with thrush are usually colonized by C. albicans during passage through the affected vagina; with an active vaginal yeast infection, the chances of development of thrush in the neonate increase.

retrieved_docs.title: Candida auris -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The mortality rate of invasive infections associated with C. auris is comparatively higher than that of other Candida species. The crude mortality rate associated with C. auris infections ranges from 30% to 72%. [11] [62] [63] [64] The variable mortality rate data may be due to several factors, including the extent of the infection, age, associated risk factors, and co-morbid conditions. Infections have been reported in preterm infants to the elderly. [40] Pediatric populations showed a higher likelihood of survival. [65] Early identification of Candida auris and prompt treatment with appropriate anti-fungal regimens are associated with higher survival. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Candidiasis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Candidiasis is more prevalent in old age and infancy. In the US, about 37% of newly born babies may be affected by thrush during the first few months of life. Children using inhaled steroids also have a higher incidence of oral candidiasis. In women, it is common during pregnancy. Thrush may be the first indication of HIV infection. Thrush is universal and is more frequent in populations with poorly nourishment. Thrush occurs equally in males and females.

retrieved_docs.title: Ophthalmia Neonatorum -- Etiology -- Other Infectious Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Conjunctivitis caused by the following infectious agents is associated with these characteristics: Pseudomonas spp., gram-negative rods Context: Premature infants in neonatal intensive care, especially with indwelling devices Onset: Variable Features: Severe purulent conjunctivitis, high risk of systemic spread Complications: Conjunctival and corneal ulceration, sepsis [20] Fungal (Candida spp.) Context: Extremely premature or immunocompromised neonates Onset: Variable, often >7 days Features: White pseudomembranes on the conjunctiva, minimal discharge Complications: Membrane formation, risk of systemic candidiasis [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Endophthalmitis -- Epidemiology -- Neonatal Endophthalmitis
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal endophthalmitis is very rare. Moshfeghi et al. reported a decreasing trend of neonatal endophthalmitis in the United States and determined that the incidence rate was 0.0045% in 2006. Unlike endophthalmitis in adults, neonatal cases are overwhelmingly endogenously sourced with Candida as the most common cause. Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) is a significant risk factor for and closely associated with neonatal endophthalmitis. It is theorized that fungal infections may cause the release of proinflammatory cytokines that promote retinal neovascularization and progression of ROP. Low birth weight and prolonged hospital stay are also important risk factors. [33] [34] [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Etiology -- Bacterial and Fungal Etiologies
retrieved_docs.content: Some epidemiologic studies have noted a shift from GBS as the most common cause of neonatal meningitis in the last few decades. A recent study in China reported the most common pathogen identified in neonatal meningitis was E. coli (39%), followed by GBS (22.1%). Gram-negative bacteria were more common in preterm infants, whereas GBS was more common in term infants. [9] Data from East Asian populations have revealed an increased frequency of multidrug-resistant (MDR) organisms causing neonatal sepsis and meningitis, especially carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii . This study also reported a correlation between lower Apgar scores and the risk of MDR meningitis. [10] An additional nosocomial pathogen that has been associated with meningitis in the neonatal period is Candida sp . [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Etiology -- Bacterial and Fungal Etiologies
retrieved_docs.content: In the late-onset group, the incidence is directly related to gestational age, birth weight, and the setting where the patient presents for care. For community-acquired late-onset sepsis and meningitis, GBS and E coli remain predominant. [5] In patients in the hospital/intensive care unit at the time of presentation, the most common pathogens are coagulase-negative staphylococci and Staphylococcus aureus , followed by E coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae . [2] [5] [8] Empiric antibiotic therapy for late-onset illness should cover additional organisms in the nosocomial environment, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa and methicillin-resistant S aureus . Late-onset meningitis is a more frequent complication of neonatal sepsis than during other periods.

retrieved_docs.title: Candidemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Candidemia is a public health concern because it is associated with poor patient outcomes, longer hospital stays, increased healthcare costs, and carries an attributable mortality rate that varies from 35% to 70%. [1] [6] Results from a recent study reported the 30-day overall mortality associated with candidemia to be 64%. [12] C andida spp are commensal yeasts that are part of normal human skin, mucosal, intestinal microbiota, and mycobiome, but they can become pathogenic and opportunistic if the appropriate circumstances are present. [1] The human mycobiome makes up less than 0.1% of the intestinal microbiome. Candida spp are a part of this biome, and up to 60% of people can be colonized with Candida spp. [2] Candida spp can become pathogenic when the equilibrium between commensal organisms is disturbed, and risk factors for Candida overgrowth, invasiveness, and translocation of the organism are present. This combination of colonization and the presence of risk factors can lead to invasive and deep-seated candidiasis, which includes candidemia. [3] . Risk factors predisposing one to the development of infection include critical illness, prolonged intensive care unit stay, an indwelling central venous catheter, cancer, transplantation, exposure to antibiotics, abdominal surgery, immunosuppression, malignancy, and total parenteral nutrition. [13] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal HIV -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of HIV infection in neonates and pediatric populations occur as a result of their immunocompromised status. They are at greater risk for opportunistic infections, candida esophagitis, Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, and cancers. Furthermore, complications are more likely to occur with antiretroviral drug resistance. However, with careful monitoring and drug-resistance testing, the ability to select more optimized and effective regimens is possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Malassezia Furfur -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recently, studies have evaluated the effectiveness of culture-independent-based methods (e.g., molecular techniques such as PCR) as a modality to assess Malassezia population epidemiology as opposed to conventional culture-based methods. Both techniques have advantages and drawbacks. Whereas culture-based methods offer the ability to discern specific virulence factors, molecular procedures allow rapid, accurate identification and quantification of Malassezia spp. The latter has helped isolate M. furfur in deep-seated infections in immunocompromised patients and preterm infants in the neonatal intensive care unit who received lipid-laden nutrition via catheters. The use of these techniques in the future appears promising, but they are not currently available for clinical use. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Candidiasis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Candidal species cause oral candidiasis when a patient's host immunity becomes disrupted. This disruption can be local, secondary to oral corticosteroid use. Overgrowth of the fungus then leads to the formation of a pseudomembrane. Vaginal infections can colonize neonates as they pass through the birth canal. Alternatively, neonates and infants may contract the disease through colonized breasts when breastfeeding. A patient's oral Candida infection can often lead to GI involvement and subsequent candidal diaper dermatitis. Candidal species thrive in moist environments. As such, females can develop vaginal candidiasis as well.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Etiology -- Bacterial and Fungal Etiologies
retrieved_docs.content: Citrobacter sp are noted to cause invasive disease in neonates and young infants, often with no risk factors. These pathogens are associated with a very high rate of brain abscesses (up to 75%) in patients with meningitis and subsequent central nervous system-related morbidity and mortality. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Candida auris -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications of invasive Candida auris infection vary depending on the extent of the infection, host co-morbidities, and resistance patterns. While the most common presentation of Candida auris infection occurs as bloodstream infection (fungemia), it may spread hematogenously to seed different organs and cause multi-organ dysfunction. Conversely, a localized infection may eventually become an overwhelming bloodstream infection and have further complications such as sepsis, multi-organ system failure involving the kidneys, heart, lungs, eyes, brain, liver, and spleen, and ultimately death.

retrieved_docs.title: Nosocomial Infections -- Etiology -- Fungi
retrieved_docs.content: Fungal pathogens are typically opportunistic, affecting immunocompromised individuals and those with indwelling devices such as central lines or urinary catheters. Candida species, such as C albicans, C parapsilosis, and C glabrata, are the most common fungal agents. [1] C auris , an emerging multidrug-resistant species, poses a significant global concern due to diagnostic challenges, high morbidity, and frequent treatment failures. [11] Collectively, Candida species rank as the fourth most common pathogen in HAIs. [12] Aspergillus fumigatus, acquired through  airborne transmission, particularly during hospital construction, may also originate from infected patients as a primary source. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE constitute a broad class of antifungal agents employed in the treatment of skin disorders, and within this class, antifungals for topical use represent the specific subset of medications applied directly to the skin surface to manage superficial fungal infections. Recognizing that topical antifungals are a component of the larger dermatological antifungal category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the site and severity of infection.

The kožní systém (skin) is a primary anatomic site associated with mycoses, indicating that many fungal infections manifest on the skin. Terconazole may be used to prevent عدوى فطرية, offering an antifungal prophylactic option for patients at risk of skin or other fungal infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1464
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics are classified as systemic antimycotics, representing a pharmacologic class of antifungal agents used for systemic infections. Within this class, itraconazole and voriconazole are specific triazole derivative compounds that belong to the group of systemic antimycotics, exemplifying the members of this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 92
retrieved_docs.content: system, underscoring the relevance of antifungal agents such as terconazole for cutaneous fungal disease. Clinical concerns common to both gynecological and topical sulfonamides include poisoning, adverse effects and underdosing, highlighting the need for careful dosing and monitoring across all routes of administration. Together, these entities and relationships depict a hierarchical and interconnected framework of antibacterial, antifungal and anti‑infective agents used in ophthalmologic, dermatologic and gynecologic care, their chemical origins, therapeutic classifications and safety considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE and antifungals for systemic use represent two distinct therapeutic categories within the broader class of antifungal agents. Antifungals for systemic use are employed when fungal infections require absorption and distribution throughout the body, whereas dermatological antifungals are applied topically to treat skin‑limited infections. The systemic antifungal category includes specific agents such as terbinafin, which is a member of the systemic antifungal group. Although both categories share the common goal of eradicating fungal pathogens, they differ in route of administration, site of action, and clinical indications. Recognizing the distinction between dermatological and systemic antifungal therapies is essential for selecting the appropriate treatment strategy and optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1656
retrieved_docs.content: Terconazole may be used to prevent عدوى فطرية (fungal infection).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE represent a therapeutic class that includes both ANTIFUNGALS FOR TOPICAL USE and ANTIFUNGALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. By definition, topical antifungal agents are applied directly to the skin, nails, or scalp to treat superficial fungal infections, whereas systemic antifungal agents are administered orally or intravenously to reach deeper or more extensive dermatologic sites. The inclusion of both topical and systemic formulations under the umbrella of ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE reflects the clinical reality that dermatologic fungal conditions can be managed with either local therapy for mild or localized disease or systemic therapy for more severe, widespread, or refractory infections. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of selecting the appropriate route of administration based on infection severity, pathogen susceptibility, and patient factors, ensuring comprehensive care for dermatologic fungal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1833
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFECTIVES FOR SYSTEMIC USE encompass a broad class of medications intended to treat infections throughout the body, and within this class, antimycotics for systemic use represent a specific subgroup that targets systemic fungal infections. Consequently, antimycotics for systemic use are considered a type of systemic antiinfective, highlighting their role as specialized agents used to eradicate fungal pathogens that have spread beyond localized sites. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of categorizing antimycotic agents under the larger umbrella of systemic antiinfective therapy for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.
